examples.title: Centralized Bus Arbitration Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates how a central arbiter grants bus access to devices based on priority
examples.code:
ALGORITHM CentralizedArbitration
BEGIN
  REPEAT
    FOR each device IN active_devices DO
      IF device.request = TRUE THEN
        arbiter.evaluate_priority(device.id)
      END IF
    END FOR
    
    selected_device ← get_highest_priority_device()
    
    FOR each device IN active_devices DO
      IF device.id = selected_device.id THEN
        device.grant ← TRUE
        bus_master ← device
      ELSE
        device.grant ← FALSE
      END IF
    END FOR
    
    WAIT UNTIL bus_release_signal
    bus_master ← NULL
  UNTIL system_shutdown
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm shows a central arbiter collecting requests from all devices, evaluating their priorities, and granting bus access to the highest priority device. Other devices must wait until the current master releases the bus.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of devices

examples.title: Bus Bandwidth Calculation
examples.description: Python program to calculate bus bandwidth
examples.code:
def calculate_bandwidth(bus_width_bits, clock_rate_mhz):
    """
    Calculate bus bandwidth in MB/s
    
    Args:
        bus_width_bits: Width of bus in bits (e.g., 32, 64)
        clock_rate_mhz: Clock rate in MHz
    
    Returns:
        Bandwidth in MB/s
    """
    # Bandwidth = (Width in bits × Clock rate) / 8 (for bytes)
    bandwidth_bytes_per_sec = (bus_width_bits * clock_rate_mhz * 1_000_000) / 8
    bandwidth_mb_per_sec = bandwidth_bytes_per_sec / (1024 * 1024)
    
    return bandwidth_mb_per_sec

# Example calculations
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Example 1: 32-bit bus at 100 MHz
    bw1 = calculate_bandwidth(32, 100)
    print(f"32-bit bus at 100 MHz: {bw1:.2f} MB/s")
    
    # Example 2: 64-bit bus at 200 MHz
    bw2 = calculate_bandwidth(64, 200)
    print(f"64-bit bus at 200 MHz: {bw2:.2f} MB/s")
    
    # Example 3: PCIe x16 (16 lanes, each lane ~1 GB/s)
    pcie_bandwidth = 16 * 1  # GB/s
    print(f"PCIe x16: {pcie_bandwidth} GB/s = {pcie_bandwidth * 1000:.0f} MB/s")
examples.explanation: This program calculates theoretical bus bandwidth using the formula: Bandwidth = (Bus Width × Clock Rate) / 8 bytes per second. It demonstrates how wider buses and higher clock rates increase bandwidth.

examples.title: Memory Read Operation via Bus
examples.description: C program demonstrating memory read operation across the system bus
examples.explanation: This C program simulates how a CPU reads data from memory using the system bus. It demonstrates the five steps: address placement, read signal assertion, wait for memory response, data retrieval, and signal cleanup.
examples.timeComplexity: O(w) where w is wait states inserted

examples.title: Daisy-Chain Bus Arbitration Protocol
examples.description: Shows how daisy-chain arbitration grants bus access based on device position
examples.code:
ALGORITHM DaisyChainArbitration
BEGIN
  // Device 0 has highest priority, Device N has lowest
  
  PROCEDURE GrantBus(device_id)
    IF device_id = 0 THEN
      IF device[0].request = TRUE THEN
        device[0].grant ← TRUE
        bus_master ← device[0]
        RETURN
      ELSE
        // Pass grant to next device
        GrantBus(1)
      END IF
    ELSE IF device_id < MAX_DEVICES THEN
      IF device[device_id].request = TRUE THEN
        device[device_id].grant ← TRUE
        bus_master ← device[device_id]
      ELSE
        // Pass grant to next device in chain
        GrantBus(device_id + 1)
      END IF
    ELSE
      // No device requested bus
      bus_master ← NULL
    END IF
  END PROCEDURE
  
  // Main arbitration loop
  WHILE system_running DO
    // Collect all requests
    FOR i = 0 TO MAX_DEVICES-1 DO
      device[i].request ← read_request_line(i)
    END FOR
    
    // Start daisy-chain grant process
    GrantBus(0)
    
    // Wait for bus release
    WAIT UNTIL bus_master.release = TRUE
    bus_master ← NULL
  END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: In daisy-chain arbitration, devices are connected in series with grant signals passed from one to the next. The device closest to the arbiter (Device 0) gets first chance; if it doesn't request, the grant passes to Device 1, and so on.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) in worst case where n is number of devices
